pages.text:
If one little bit was actually an ant, then eight ants would make up a byte.

If a byte of ants were found in his pants, your grandpa might dance through the night.

If one little byte was actually a bee, then a kilobyte could be a hive.

If one thousand bees were buzzing in trees an orchard’s blossoms would thrive.

If one little byte was actually a pig, then a megabyte would make up a sounder.

If a million pigs all oinked at your sister, they’d shock and alarm and astound her.

If one little byte was actually a cow, then a gigabyte would fill up a lagoon.

If one billion cows all mooed out loud, they’d wake up the man on the moon.

If one little byte was actually a tree, then a terabyte would be a grand forest.

A trillion trees, leaning with the breeze, and swaying like a towering chorus.

If one tiny byte was actually a note, then a petabyte could record all our singing.

Thousands of trillions of harmonies stored, through hums and whistles and ringing.

If one little byte was actually a grain, then an exabyte could be all beaches.

Millions of trillions of sand to walk on, encrypted and colored like peaches.

If one little byte was actually a drop, then a zettabyte would be all oceans.

Billions of trillions of droplets for fish, to swim in and practice their motions.

If one little byte was actually a cell, then a yottabyte would be all the living.

Trillions of trillions of bodies in code, each breathing, and sharing, and giving.

If one little byte was a piece of paper, then a ronnabyte would stack—what’d you say?

Thousands of trillions of trillions of feet reaching two and a half light years away!

If one little byte was the tiniest of sparks then a quettabyte would be all the light.

Millions of trillions of trillions of flashes conquering the depths of the night.

With all these bits and towering sums, our weary minds drift off to sleep.

From one little byte to conquering the night—next time we’ll just count some sheep.
